message:
fix(gf2): Fix persistence_pairs() panic on non-square boundary matrices (bd-3502)

The is_birth vector was sized by cols but indexed by pivot row indices
that can exceed cols when rows > cols (e.g., ∂₂ matrices from
SquareComplex::boundary_2()). Size is_birth by rows and guard the
lookup in the unpaired detection loop.

Added regression tests for both rows > cols and cols > rows cases.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.5 <noreply@anthropic.com>

 {"id":"bd-24c","title":"Implement Scope API (user-facing region handle)","description":"# Scope API (User-Facing Region Handle)

## Purpose
`Scope<'r>` is the user-facing handle to a region. It is the API surface that enforces structured concurrency:
- every spawned task is owned by exactly one region
- region close implies quiescence (no live children, finalizers done, obligations resolved)

The lifetime `'r` prevents handles from escaping their region.

## Core Responsibilities
- spawn work (Phase 0: single-thread fiber tier; Phase 1: Send task tier)
- create subregions
- register finalizers
- surface `Cx` capabilities and tracing

## Policy Integration (must exist)
`Scope::region_with_policy` (or equivalent) requires a `Policy` type that defines:
- how to aggregate child outcomes
- how to react to child failures (e.g., fail-fast cancels siblings)

## Key APIs (Sketch)
### spawn
```rust
pub fn spawn<F, T>(&self, f: F) -> JoinHandle<'r, T>
where
    F: Future<Output = T> + 'r,
```

### region
```rust
pub async fn region<F, Fut>(&self, f: F) -> Outcome<()>
where
    F: FnOnce(Scope<'_>) -> Fut,
    Fut: Future<Output = ()>,
```

### finalizers
- `defer_sync` / `defer_async`

### handles
`JoinHandle` supports:
- `join().await -> Outcome<T>`
- `cancel(reason)` (request cancellation; must still drain)

## “Dropping a JoinHandle” Rule
Dropping a handle **does not detach work**. The region still owns the task and will cancel/drain it on region close.

## Example (No stdout/stderr in core)
```rust
root.region(|sub| async move {
    sub.defer_sync(|cx| cx.trace_user("cleanup", TraceData::None));

    let h1 = sub.spawn(async move { 1 });
    let h2 = sub.spawn(async move { 2 });

    let _ = h1.join().await;
    let _ = h2.join().await;
}).await;
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- Lifetimes prevent escaping scopes.
- Region close waits for children/finalizers/obligations.
- Policy hooks exist for fail-fast semantics.

## Testing
- Compile-time tests where feasible (no escape).
- Lab E2E scenarios validate nested regions and quiescence.

 {"id":"bd-24hd","title":"race\! macro: first completion with loser cancellation","description":"# race\! Macro Implementation

## Goal

Implement the race\! macro for returning the first future to complete while properly cancelling and draining losers.

## Semantics

```rust
// Basic usage - returns Either-like enum
let winner: Race3<A, B, C> = race\!(fut_a, fut_b, fut_c).await;

match winner {
    Race3::First(a) => println\!('A won: {:?}', a),
    Race3::Second(b) => println\!('B won: {:?}', b),
    Race3::Third(c) => println\!('C won: {:?}', c),
}
```

## Key Properties

### 1. First Wins
First future to return Poll::Ready is the winner.

### 2. Losers Cancelled
All non-winning futures are cancelled:
- Cancel request sent
- Drain phase (bounded by budget)
- Finalize phase
- Obligations resolved

**This is the CRITICAL difference from other race implementations.** Losers are not simply dropped - they go through the full cancellation protocol.

### 3. Wait for Drain
race\! does NOT return until all losers have completed cancellation:
```rust
// Pseudocode
loop {
    // Poll all pending
    for i in 0..futures.len() {
        if \!completed[i] {
            match futures[i].poll(cx) {
                Poll::Ready(outcome) => {
                    if winner.is_none() {
                        winner = Some((i, outcome));
                        // Cancel all others
                        for j in 0..futures.len() {
                            if j \!= i && \!completed[j] {
                                futures[j].cancel(CancelReason::RaceLost);
                            }
                        }
                    }
                    completed[i] = true;
                }
                Poll::Pending => {}
            }
        }
    }
    
    if completed.iter().all(|&c| c) {
        break; // All done, including losers
    }
}
```

### 4. Loser Outcomes
Losers complete with Outcome::Cancelled(RaceLost). Their outcomes are available for inspection:
```rust
let result = race\!(fut_a, fut_b).await;
assert\!(result.loser_outcomes().all(|o| o.is_cancelled()));
```

### 5. Cancellation Propagation
If parent region is cancelled:
- ALL futures cancelled (including current 'winner')
- race returns Cancelled outcome

## Implementation

### Race Enum
```rust
pub enum Race2<A, B> {
    First(A),
    Second(B),
}

pub enum Race3<A, B, C> {
    First(A),
    Second(B),
    Third(C),
}
// ... up to Race16
```

### Race Future
```rust
pub struct RaceFuture2<F1, F2> {
    fut1: MaybeCancelling<F1>,
    fut2: MaybeCancelling<F2>,
    winner: Option<usize>,
}

enum MaybeCancelling<F: Future> {
    Running(F),
    Cancelling(F),
    Done(Outcome<F::Output, Error>),
}

impl<F1, F2> Future for RaceFuture2<F1, F2>
where
    F1: Future + Cancel,
    F2: Future + Cancel,
{
    type Output = Race2<F1::Output, F2::Output>;
    
    fn poll(self: Pin<&mut Self>, cx: &mut Context<'_>) -> Poll<Self::Output> {
        // 1. Poll all non-done futures
        // 2. If any completes and no winner yet, mark winner and cancel others
        // 3. When ALL done (including cancelled losers), return winner
    }
}
```

### Cancel Trait
Futures in race\! must implement Cancel:
```rust
pub trait Cancel: Future {
    /// Initiate cancellation
    fn cancel(&mut self, reason: CancelReason);
    
    /// Check if cancelled
    fn is_cancelled(&self) -> bool;
}
```

## Macro Syntax

```rust
// Basic
race\!(a, b, c)

// With biased polling (first listed polled first)
race\! { biased;
    a,
    b,
    c,
}

// Named (for clarity)
race\! {
    cache = check_cache(key),
    db = query_database(key),
}
```

## Biased vs Unbiased

- **Unbiased**: Random polling order (deterministic with seed in lab)
- **Biased**: Left-to-right polling order (useful for fallback patterns)

## Testing

- Basic race of 2 futures
- Verify loser is cancelled
- Verify loser fully drained before return
- Race under parent cancellation
- Race with panicking future
- Biased vs unbiased behavior
- Obligation cleanup in losers

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] race\! macro for 2-16 futures
- [ ] RaceN enum types
- [ ] RaceFutureN implementations
- [ ] Cancel trait
- [ ] Full loser drain (not just drop)
- [ ] Loser outcome inspection
- [ ] Biased/unbiased modes
- [ ] Tests for all scenarios","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:30:46.355124531Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T08:15:31.384824034Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T08:15:31.384655390Z","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["combinators","core","race"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-24hd","depends_on_id":"bd-14c0","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:06Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-24hd","depends_on_id":"bd-1sx3","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:06Z","created_by":"import"}]}

 {"id":"bd-257b","title":"Obligation tracker: real-time view of held obligations and potential leaks","description":"# Obligation Tracker

## Overview
Real-time dashboard for tracking all obligations (permits, leases, acks) across the
runtime, with leak detection and aging warnings.

## Design

### Obligation Overview
```rust
pub struct ObligationTracker {
    state: Arc<RuntimeState>,
    console: Console,
}

impl ObligationTracker {
    /// List all active obligations.
    pub fn list_obligations(&self) -> Vec<ObligationInfo> {
        self.state.all_obligations()
            .map(|o| ObligationInfo {
                id: o.id(),
                type_name: o.type_name(),
                holder_task: o.holder_task(),
                holder_region: o.holder_region(),
                created_at: o.created_at(),
                age: o.age(),
                state: o.state(),
            })
            .collect()
    }
    
    /// Find potentially leaked obligations.
    pub fn find_potential_leaks(&self, age_threshold: Duration) -> Vec<ObligationInfo> {
        self.list_obligations()
            .into_iter()
            .filter(|o| o.age > age_threshold && o.state.is_active())
            .collect()
    }
    
    /// Get obligations by type.
    pub fn by_type(&self, type_name: &str) -> Vec<ObligationInfo> {
        self.list_obligations()
            .into_iter()
            .filter(|o| o.type_name == type_name)
            .collect()
    }
}
```

### Obligation Types Tracked
- **ConnectionLease**: Database/network connections
- **TransactionLock**: Uncommitted transactions
- **ChannelPermit**: Bounded channel send permits
- **MessageAck**: Unacknowledged queue messages
- **FileLock**: File reservation leases
- **TimerHandle**: Active timer registrations

### Output Format
```
Obligation Tracker
╭─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╮
│ Active Obligations: 12  │  Potential Leaks: 2  │  Age > 1m: 5 │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ Type              Count  Oldest    Holder                   │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ConnectionLease     5    2.3s      Region-2 (HttpHandler)   │
│ TransactionLock     2    45.2s     Task-42 (db_update)      │
│ MessageAck          3    12.1s     Task-17 (process_queue)  │
│ ChannelPermit       2    0.1s      Task-89 (sender)         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ⚠️  POTENTIAL LEAKS (age > 60s):                            │
│   TransactionLock held by Task-42 for 45.2s                 │
│     -> Created at: src/db/queries.rs:89                     │
│     -> Recommendation: Check for missing commit/rollback    │
╰─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────╯
```

### Interactive Features (TUI)
- Filter by obligation type
- Sort by age, holder, type
- Click to drill down
- Threshold alerts configuration

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] List all active obligations
- [ ] Group by type
- [ ] Age tracking
- [ ] Leak detection with thresholds
- [ ] Holder information
- [ ] Creation location tracking
- [ ] TUI dashboard
- [ ] Unit tests

## Testing Requirements

### Unit Tests
- `tracker::tests::list_all_obligations` - Enumerate obligations
- `tracker::tests::filter_by_type` - Type filtering
- `tracker::tests::detect_potential_leak` - Leak detection
- `tracker::tests::age_threshold_filter` - Age-based alerts
- `tracker::tests::holder_info_display` - Show holder details

### Integration Tests
- `tracker::integration::live_obligations` - Real obligations
- `tracker::integration::concurrent_create_release` - Concurrent ops

### Logging Requirements
- TRACE: Individual obligation lookups
- DEBUG: Obligation list queries
- INFO: Potential leak warnings
- WARN: Long-held obligations","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-01T02:58:03.582168798Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-01T08:02:03.822599882Z","closed_at":"2026-02-01T08:02:03.822527798Z","close_reason":"Implemented ObligationTracker with: list_obligations(), find_potential_leaks(), by_type/by_task/by_region filters, summary(), and render_summary().  {"id":"bd-2581","title":"Supervision trees: policies, strategies, restart backoff","description":"# Supervision Trees

## Goal

Implement supervisor actors that monitor children and apply restart policies on failure.

## Background

Supervision is the Erlang/Akka pattern for fault tolerance: parent actors watch children and decide what to do when they fail. This transforms crashes from disasters into routine events.

## Restart Policies

### OneForOne
- Only the failed child is restarted
- Other children unaffected
- Use when children are independent

### OneForAll
- All children restarted when one fails
- Use when children have shared state dependencies

### RestForOne
- Failed child and all children started after it are restarted
- Use when children have ordered dependencies

### Custom
- User-defined Decide trait implementation
- Full control over restart logic

## SupervisionConfig

```rust
pub struct SupervisionConfig {
    /// Restart policy to apply
    pub policy: RestartPolicy,
    
    /// Max restarts in time window before escalating
    pub max_restarts: u32,
    
    /// Time window for counting restarts
    pub restart_window: Duration,
    
    /// Backoff strategy between restarts
    pub backoff: BackoffStrategy,
    
    /// What to do when max_restarts exceeded
    pub escalation: EscalationPolicy,
}
```

## BackoffStrategy

```rust
pub enum BackoffStrategy {
    /// No delay between restarts
    Immediate,
    
    /// Fixed delay between restarts
    Fixed(Duration),
    
    /// Exponential backoff with jitter
    Exponential {
        initial: Duration,
        max: Duration,
        multiplier: f64,
    },
}
```

## EscalationPolicy

What happens when max_restarts exceeded:
1. **Stop**: Stop the failing actor permanently
2. **Escalate**: Propagate failure to parent supervisor
3. **Restart**: Reset restart counter and try again

## Supervisor Actor

```rust
pub struct Supervisor {
    children: HashMap<ActorId, ChildSpec>,
    config: SupervisionConfig,
    restart_history: VecDeque<Time>,
}

pub struct ChildSpec {
    actor: Box<dyn Actor>,
    config: SupervisionConfig,
    mailbox_config: MailboxConfig,
}
```

## Failure Detection

Supervisor is notified when:
1. Child returns Outcome::Err from receive()
2. Child returns Outcome::Panicked
3. Child stops unexpectedly (not requested by parent)

## Implementation Notes

- Supervisor is itself an actor (supervisors can supervise supervisors)
- Root supervisor created by runtime
- Restart history uses circular buffer for efficiency
- Backoff implemented via Cx::sleep

## Logging & Observability

Every supervision event traced:
- child_failed(actor_id, reason)
- child_restarting(actor_id, attempt, delay)
- child_restarted(actor_id, attempt)
- max_restarts_exceeded(actor_id, escalation)

## Testing

- OneForOne: verify only failed child restarts
- OneForAll: verify all children restart
- RestForOne: verify correct subset restarts
- Backoff: verify delays between restarts
- Max restarts: verify escalation triggers
- Lab runtime: deterministic supervision behavior

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] RestartPolicy enum with 4 policies
- [ ] SupervisionConfig with all options
- [ ] BackoffStrategy with 3 strategies
- [ ] EscalationPolicy with 3 options
- [ ] Supervisor actor implementation
- [ ] Failure detection from child outcomes
- [ ] Restart history tracking
- [ ] Tracing for all supervision events","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-01-31T21:18:44.425130610Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T01:03:29.168789271Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T01:03:29.168670901Z","close_reason":"Added RestartPolicy (OneForOne/OneForAll/RestForOne), EscalationPolicy (Stop/Escalate/ResetCounter), SupervisionConfig, ChildSpec. 10 new tests.","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["actors","phase3","supervision"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-2581","depends_on_id":"bd-3dcp","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:06Z","created_by":"import"},{"issue_id":"bd-2581","depends_on_id":"bd-3t9g","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-03T00:02:06Z","created_by":"import"}]}

-{"id":"bd-28sb","title":"[ALIEN][TRACE][GEO] Exact solver for bounded traces (A*/IDA*)","description":"## Purpose

Compute *optimal* minimum-switch linear extensions for bounded traces.

This is used for:
- small counterexamples
- producing canonical minimal-entropy replay traces

## Algorithm sketch

State = set of executed nodes + current owner.
- Available set = minimal elements not yet executed.
- Transition = pick one available node.
- Cost = +1 if owner changes.

Use A* or IDA*:
- g = switches so far
- h = admissible lower bound on remaining switches (e.g., 0; or count of distinct owners remaining minus 1 in best case, carefully bounded)

## Determinism

- Priority queue ordering is deterministic: (f,g,tie_break).
- tie_break uses event key and stable owner ordering.

## Acceptance criteria

 {"id":"bd-293f","title":"Epic: Scheduler Performance & Fairness Improvements","description":"# Epic: Scheduler Performance & Fairness Improvements

## Overview

Beyond critical bugs, the scheduler has several performance and fairness 
issues that impact throughput, latency, and predictability under load.

## Background & Context

The scheduler's performance characteristics directly impact:
- **Throughput**: Tasks processed per second
- **Latency**: Time from task ready to execution
- **Fairness**: Equal treatment of equal-priority tasks
- **Efficiency**: CPU cycles per task

## Performance Issues

1. **PriorityScheduler O(n) insert** (`priority.rs:27-43`)
   - Linear scan for insertion position
   - Degrades with large queues

2. **Imprecise wakeup strategy** (`three_lane.rs:127-134`)
   - Always wakes first parker
   - No load balancing consideration

3. **Try-lock contention in stealing** (`three_lane.rs:367`)
   - Multiple stealers contend on same lock
   - Wasted CPU cycles

## Fairness Issues

4. **RNG tie-breaking bias** (`priority.rs:27-70`)
   - Single RNG hint used for all decisions
   - Predictable, non-random selection

5. **Duplicate scheduling across queues** (`priority.rs:131-135`)
   - Task can be in both local and global queue
   - Potential double execution

6. **Only stealing from ready lane** (`three_lane.rs:354-387`)
   - Cancel/timed work not stolen
   - Uneven work distribution

## Performance Goals

- O(1) or O(log n) operations on hot path
- Minimal lock contention
- Efficient work distribution
- Predictable latency

## Success Criteria

- Benchmark suite for scheduler operations
- Throughput improvement measurable
- Latency distribution documented
- Fairness verified statistically","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-01-31T20:58:38.139343833Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-02T03:55:17.583483511Z","closed_at":"2026-02-02T03:55:17.583408902Z","close_reason":"done","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["runtime","scheduler"]}

 {"id":"bd-35f9","title":"Scheduler: prevent duplicate scheduling across local/global queues","description":"# Bug: Task Can Be Scheduled in Both Local and Global Queue

## Location
`src/runtime/scheduler/priority.rs:131-135`
`src/runtime/scheduler/global_injector.rs` (inject_ready)

## Issue Description

Deduplication only works within a single `PriorityScheduler` instance:

```rust
pub fn schedule(&mut self, task: TaskId, priority: u8) {
    if self.scheduled.insert(task) {  // Only prevents duplicates locally
        insert_by_priority(&mut self.ready_lane, SchedulerEntry { task, priority });
    }
}
```

But tasks can be injected via multiple paths:
- `schedule_local()` → local PriorityScheduler
- `inject_ready()` → GlobalInjector

No cross-queue deduplication exists.

## Duplicate Scheduling Scenario

```
1. Task T1 calls spawn() on task T2
2. T2 injected to local scheduler (fast path)
3. Before T2 executes, T1's waker wakes T2 again
4. T2 injected to global queue (waker path)
5. T2 now in BOTH local and global queues
6. Worker A pops T2 from local, executes
7. Worker B steals T2 from global, executes
8. T2 executed twice!
```

## Impact

- **Severity**: HIGH (correctness violation)
- **Exploitability**: Triggered by concurrent wake patterns
- **Impact**: Double execution, potential use-after-free

## Root Cause

No centralized "is this task scheduled?" check. Each queue maintains 
its own set, but they don't communicate.

## Proposed Fix

Centralized scheduling state in the task record:

```rust
enum TaskScheduleState {
    NotScheduled,
    ScheduledLocal(WorkerId),
    ScheduledGlobal,
    Running(WorkerId),
    Completed,
}

struct TaskRecord {
    // ...
    schedule_state: AtomicU8,  // Or use enum with AtomicCell
}

fn try_schedule(&self, task_id: TaskId, target: ScheduleTarget) -> bool {
    let record = self.get_task(task_id);
    // CAS from NotScheduled to Scheduled
    record.schedule_state.compare_exchange(
        NotScheduled,
        target.to_state(),
        Ordering::AcqRel,
        Ordering::Acquire,
    ).is_ok()
}
```

---

#[test]
fn stress_concurrent_wakes_single_execution() {
    init_test_logging();
    
    let scheduler = Arc::new(Scheduler::new(4));
    let exec_counts = Arc::new(Mutex::new(HashMap::new()));
    
    // Create tasks that can be woken concurrently
    let tasks: Vec<TaskId> = (0..1000).map(|_| {
        scheduler.spawn(|| {
            std::thread::sleep(Duration::from_micros(100));
        })
    }).collect();
    
    // Concurrently wake all tasks multiple times
    let handles: Vec<_> = (0..10).map(|_| {
        let sched = Arc::clone(&scheduler);
        let task_ids = tasks.clone();
        thread::spawn(move || {
            for &task in &task_ids {
                sched.wake(task);
            }
        })
    }).collect();
    
    for h in handles { h.join().unwrap(); }
    scheduler.wait_idle();
    
    // Verify each task executed exactly once
    let counts = exec_counts.lock().unwrap();
    for task in &tasks {
        let count = counts.get(task).copied().unwrap_or(0);
        assert_eq!(count, 1, "task {:?} executed {} times, expected 1", task, count);
    }
}

        let poll_count = Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(0));
        
        let wc = Arc::clone(&wake_count);
        let pc = Arc::clone(&poll_count);
        
        let task = scheduler.spawn(move |waker: &Waker| {
            pc.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst);
            
            // Wake self during poll
            if wc.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst) < 10 {
                waker.wake_by_ref();
                Poll::Pending
            } else {
                Poll::Ready(())
            }
        });
        
        scheduler.wait_task(task);
        
        // Should poll exactly 11 times (10 pending + 1 ready)
        assert_eq!(poll_count.load(Ordering::SeqCst), 11);
    }
}

### Loom Concurrency Tests (tests/scheduler/dedup_loom.rs)

```rust
use loom::sync::{Arc, atomic::{AtomicU8, AtomicUsize, Ordering}};
use loom::thread;

const NOT_SCHEDULED: u8 = 0;
const SCHEDULED: u8 = 1;
const RUNNING: u8 = 2;

#[test]
fn loom_schedule_race() {
    loom::model(|| {
        let state = Arc::new(AtomicU8::new(NOT_SCHEDULED));
        let scheduled_count = Arc::new(AtomicUsize::new(0));
        
        // Two threads try to schedule
        let s1 = Arc::clone(&state);
        let c1 = Arc::clone(&scheduled_count);
        let t1 = thread::spawn(move || {
            if s1.compare_exchange(NOT_SCHEDULED, SCHEDULED, Ordering::AcqRel, Ordering::Acquire).is_ok() {
                c1.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
                true
            } else {
                false
            }
        });
        
        let s2 = Arc::clone(&state);
        let c2 = Arc::clone(&scheduled_count);
        let t2 = thread::spawn(move || {
            if s2.compare_exchange(NOT_SCHEDULED, SCHEDULED, Ordering::AcqRel, Ordering::Acquire).is_ok() {
                c2.fetch_add(1, Ordering::Relaxed);
                true
            } else {
                false
            }
        });
        
        let r1 = t1.join().unwrap();
        let r2 = t2.join().unwrap();
        
        // Exactly one should succeed
        assert_ne!(r1, r2, "both returned same value");
        assert_eq!(scheduled_count.load(Ordering::Relaxed), 1);
    });
}

#[test]
fn loom_wake_during_poll() {
    loom::model(|| {
        let state = Arc::new(AtomicU8::new(RUNNING));
        let wake_pending = Arc::new(AtomicBool::new(false));
        
        // Poller thread
        let s1 = Arc::clone(&state);
        let w1 = Arc::clone(&wake_pending);
        let poller = thread::spawn(move || {
            // Finish poll
            if w1.swap(false, Ordering::AcqRel) {
                // Wake was pending, reschedule
                s1.store(SCHEDULED, Ordering::Release);
                true
            } else {
                s1.store(NOT_SCHEDULED, Ordering::Release);
                false
            }
        });
        
        // Waker thread
        let s2 = Arc::clone(&state);
        let w2 = Arc::clone(&wake_pending);
        let waker = thread::spawn(move || {
            if s2.load(Ordering::Acquire) == RUNNING {
                // Task is running, set pending flag
                w2.store(true, Ordering::Release);
                true
            } else if s2.compare_exchange(NOT_SCHEDULED, SCHEDULED, Ordering::AcqRel, Ordering::Acquire).is_ok() {
                // Was not scheduled, schedule now
                true
            } else {
                false
            }
        });
        
        let rescheduled = poller.join().unwrap();
        let woke = waker.join().unwrap();
        
        // Wake should have effect - either rescheduled or pending
        // No wake should be lost
    });
}

#[test]
fn loom_local_global_race() {
    loom::model(|| {
        let state = Arc::new(AtomicU8::new(NOT_SCHEDULED));
        let local_queue = Arc::new(Mutex::new(Vec::new()));
        let global_queue = Arc::new(Mutex::new(Vec::new()));
        
        // Local injector
        let s1 = Arc::clone(&state);
        let l1 = Arc::clone(&local_queue);
        let t1 = thread::spawn(move || {
            if s1.compare_exchange(NOT_SCHEDULED, SCHEDULED, Ordering::AcqRel, Ordering::Acquire).is_ok() {
                l1.lock().unwrap().push(42);
            }
        });
        
        // Global injector
        let s2 = Arc::clone(&state);
        let g2 = Arc::clone(&global_queue);
        let t2 = thread::spawn(move || {
            if s2.compare_exchange(NOT_SCHEDULED, SCHEDULED, Ordering::AcqRel, Ordering::Acquire).is_ok() {
                g2.lock().unwrap().push(42);
            }
        });
        
        t1.join().unwrap();
        t2.join().unwrap();
        
        // Task should be in exactly one queue
        let in_local = !local_queue.lock().unwrap().is_empty();
        let in_global = !global_queue.lock().unwrap().is_empty();
        assert!(in_local ^ in_global, "task in both or neither queue");
    });
}

## Overview

## Test Directory Structure
```

## Test Categories

### 1. Conformance Tests (RFC 6455)

### 2. Cancel-Correctness Tests
- Client Cx cancelled mid-send

### 3. Integration Tests
- Echo server round-trip
- Multi-client chat broadcast
- Binary file transfer (large messages)
- Reconnection after network failure

### 4. Lab Runtime Tests
- Deterministic replay of WebSocket interactions

## Logging Requirements

### Log Levels
- TRACE: Frame-level details (opcode, length, first 64 bytes)
- DEBUG: Message-level (send/recv, size)
- INFO: Connection lifecycle (connect, close, error)
- WARN: Protocol anomalies (unexpected frames)
- ERROR: Failures with context

tracing::info\!(

    message_count = count,
    bytes_transferred = bytes,
    close_code = ?code,
    duration_ms = elapsed.as_millis(),

);

### Artifact Collection

- Trace event timeline
- Frame dump on failure

 {"id":"bd-3z8z","title":"[SUB-EPIC] macOS Reactor (kqueue)","description":"# Sub-Epic: macOS Reactor (kqueue)

## Purpose

Implement a production-quality kqueue-based reactor for macOS and BSD systems. Important for developer experience since many developers use Macs.

## Background

### What is kqueue?

kqueue (kernel event queue) is the BSD/macOS event notification mechanism. Similar to epoll in goals, but different API and semantics.

Key syscalls:
- `kqueue()` - Create kqueue instance
- `kevent()` - Register interest and wait for events (combined operation)

### kqueue vs epoll

| Feature | epoll (Linux) | kqueue (BSD/macOS) |
|---------|---------------|-------------------|
| Level/Edge | Both, flag | Edge by default |
| Add/Wait | Separate calls | Combined in kevent() |
| Multiple events | No | Yes (one call) |
| Timer support | timerfd | Built-in |
| Signal support | signalfd | Built-in |

### Edge-Triggered Behavior

kqueue is edge-triggered by default:
- EV_CLEAR flag re-arms after delivery (like edge-triggered)
- Without EV_CLEAR, event is disabled after delivery (one-shot)

We'll use EV_CLEAR for persistent monitoring.

## Design

```rust
pub struct KqueueReactor {
    /// The kqueue file descriptor
    kq_fd: RawFd,
    /// Token slab and state
    inner: Mutex<KqueueInner>,
}

struct KqueueInner {
    wakers: TokenSlab,
    /// Track registered fds to prevent double-registration
    registrations: HashSet<(RawFd, i16)>, // (fd, filter)
}
```

## Key Differences from Epoll Implementation

1. **Combined register + wait**: kevent() does both
2. **Filters**: EVFILT_READ, EVFILT_WRITE (not flags)
3. **User data**: udata field for token (like epoll_event.data)
4. **Wakeup**: Use pipe or EVFILT_USER (macOS 10.6+)

## Platform Requirements

- macOS 10.6+ (for EVFILT_USER)
- Or FreeBSD, OpenBSD, NetBSD, DragonFlyBSD

## Deliverables

1. KqueueReactor implementing Reactor trait
2. EVFILT_USER wakeup mechanism (or pipe fallback)
3. EV_CLEAR for persistent monitoring
4. Unit tests with real sockets
5. Cross-platform test coverage with Linux

## Success Criteria

- [ ] Feature parity with EpollReactor
- [ ] Works on macOS Ventura+
- [ ] Cross-thread wakeup reliable
- [ ] All epoll tests have kqueue equivalents","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"feature","owner":"jeff141421@gmail.com","created_at":"2026-01-18T05:43:22.548330212Z","created_by":"Dicklesworthstone","updated_at":"2026-01-20T22:15:50.510890807Z","closed_at":"2026-01-20T22:15:50.510087003Z","close_reason":"All dependencies complete: wx8h (Core Reactor Abstraction), cysj (register/deregister/poll/wake), and s24w (core KqueueReactor structure) are now CLOSED.  {"id":"bd-41lt","title":"Create Lab reactor determinism tests","description":"# Task: Create Lab Reactor Determinism Tests

## What

Tests that verify the Lab reactor provides deterministic execution, enabling reproducible concurrent tests.

## Location

`tests/lab_determinism.rs` (new file)

## Determinism Principle

Same seed → Same execution → Same results

This is critical for debugging concurrent bugs - if a test fails, the same seed reproduces the exact failure.

## Tests

### Basic Determinism

```rust
#[test]
fn test_lab_deterministic_scheduling() {
    let seed = 42;
    
    // Run twice with same seed
    let result1 = run_with_seed(seed);
    let result2 = run_with_seed(seed);
    
    // Must produce identical results
    assert_eq!(result1, result2);
}

fn run_with_seed(seed: u64) -> Vec<usize> {
    let lab = LabRuntime::new_with_seed(seed);
    let results = Rc::new(RefCell::new(Vec::new()));
    
    lab.block_on(async {
        let results = results.clone();
        
        // Spawn tasks that race
        let tasks: Vec<_> = (0..10).map(|i| {
            let r = results.clone();
            cx.spawn(async move {
                r.borrow_mut().push(i);
            })
        }).collect();
        
        // Wait for all
        for task in tasks {
            task.await;
        }
    });
    
    results.borrow().clone()
}
```

### I/O Event Determinism

```rust
#[test]
fn test_lab_io_event_ordering() {
    let seed = 123;
    
    let result1 = run_io_test(seed);
    let result2 = run_io_test(seed);
    
    assert_eq!(result1, result2);
}

fn run_io_test(seed: u64) -> Vec<String> {
    let lab = LabRuntime::new_with_seed(seed);
    let reactor = lab.reactor();
    let events = Rc::new(RefCell::new(Vec::new()));
    
    lab.block_on(async {
        // Create fake sockets
        let (s1, s2) = create_lab_socket_pair();
        
        // Schedule I/O events
        reactor.schedule_event(s1.token(), Interest::READABLE, Duration::from_millis(10));
        reactor.schedule_event(s2.token(), Interest::READABLE, Duration::from_millis(10));
        
        // Both events at same time - order must be deterministic
        
        // ... capture which fires first ...
    });
    
    events.borrow().clone()
}
```

### Fault Injection Determinism

```rust
#[test]
fn test_lab_fault_injection_determinism() {
    let seed = 456;
    
    // Configure same faults
    let config = FaultConfig {
        read_fail_probability: 0.3,
        error_kind: io::ErrorKind::ConnectionReset,
        ..Default::default()
    };
    
    let failures1 = count_failures_with_seed(seed, config.clone());
    let failures2 = count_failures_with_seed(seed, config);
    
    // Same number of failures, at same operations
    assert_eq!(failures1, failures2);
}
```

### Time Advancement Determinism

```rust
#[test]
fn test_lab_virtual_time_determinism() {
    let seed = 789;
    
    let events1 = run_timed_test(seed);
    let events2 = run_timed_test(seed);
    
    assert_eq!(events1, events2);
}

fn run_timed_test(seed: u64) -> Vec<(Time, String)> {
    let lab = LabRuntime::new_with_seed(seed);
    let events = Rc::new(RefCell::new(Vec::new()));
    
    lab.block_on(async {
        // Spawn timer tasks
        for i in 0..5 {
            let e = events.clone();
            cx.spawn(async move {
                sleep(Duration::from_millis(i * 10)).await;
                e.borrow_mut().push((Cx::current().now(), format!("task-{}", i)));
            });
        }
        
        // Advance time
        lab.advance_time(Duration::from_secs(1));
        lab.run_until_quiescent();
    });
    
    events.borrow().clone()
}
```

### Trace Capture Consistency

```rust
#[test]
fn test_trace_capture_determinism() {
    let seed = 101112;
    
    let trace1 = capture_trace_with_seed(seed);
    let trace2 = capture_trace_with_seed(seed);
    
    // Traces should be identical (same events in same order)
    assert_eq!(trace1.len(), trace2.len());
    for (e1, e2) in trace1.iter().zip(trace2.iter()) {
        assert_eq!(e1, e2);
    }
}
```

### Different Seeds → Different Execution

```rust
#[test]
fn test_different_seeds_different_results() {
    // Different seeds should produce different orderings
    let result1 = run_with_seed(1);
    let result2 = run_with_seed(2);
    let result3 = run_with_seed(3);
    
    // At least some should differ (probabilistically)
    // (If all 10 tasks execute in same order for all seeds, something's wrong)
    let all_same = result1 == result2 && result2 == result3;
    assert!(!all_same, "Different seeds should produce different orderings");
}
```

## Test Utilities

```rust
/// Helper to run test multiple times and verify consistency
fn verify_deterministic<F, T>(seed: u64, runs: usize, f: F)
where
    F: Fn(u64) -> T,
    T: Eq + std::fmt::Debug,
{
    let baseline = f(seed);
    for _ in 1..runs {
        let result = f(seed);
        assert_eq!(result, baseline, "Non-deterministic execution detected");
    }
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Same seed produces same task ordering
- [ ] Same seed produces same I/O event ordering  
- [ ] Same seed produces same fault injection pattern
- [ ] Traces are identical for same seed
- [ ] Different seeds produce different execution

 {"id":"bd-4d8m","title":"Implement Tower feature flag and optional dependency","description":"## Overview

Make Tower integration optional via feature flag to avoid mandatory dependencies.

## Requirements

### Feature Flag
```toml
# Cargo.toml
[features]
default = []
tower = ["dep:tower", "dep:tower-service"]

[dependencies]
tower = { version = "0.4", optional = true }
tower-service = { version = "0.3", optional = true }
```

### Conditional Compilation
```rust
// src/tower/mod.rs
#![cfg(feature = "tower")]

mod adapter;
mod service;

pub use adapter::{AsupersyncAdapter, TowerAdapter};
pub use service::AsupersyncService;
```

### Re-exports
```rust
// src/lib.rs
#[cfg(feature = "tower")]
pub mod tower;
```

Document feature in:
- README.md
- lib.rs module docs
- Cargo.toml metadata

1. "tower" feature flag

 {"id":"bd-4k7","title":"Implement test oracle: losers_always_drained invariant checker","description":"## Purpose
Implement a test oracle that verifies the "losers always drained" invariant: in any race/select operation, losing tasks are cancelled AND fully drained before the operation returns.

## The Invariant
From asupersync_plan_v4.md:
> Races must cancel and fully drain losers

This prevents resource leaks, ensures cleanup runs, and maintains predictable behavior.

## What "Drained" Means
A task is drained when it has:
1. Received cancel request (`CancelRequested` state)
2. Run to next checkpoint (`Cancelling` state)
3. Executed finalizers (`Finalizing` state)
4. Reached `Completed(Cancelled)` state

Simply requesting cancellation is NOT sufficient. The task must actually complete.

## Oracle Design

```rust
pub struct LoserDrainOracle {
    // Tracks race/select operations
    race_starts: Vec<RaceEvent>,
    race_completes: Vec<RaceCompletion>,
    task_drains: HashMap<TaskId, DrainRecord>,
}

pub struct RaceEvent {
    pub race_id: RaceId,
    pub participants: Vec<TaskId>,
    pub start_time: Time,
}

pub struct RaceCompletion {
    pub race_id: RaceId,
    pub winner: TaskId,
    pub losers: Vec<TaskId>,
    pub complete_time: Time,
}

pub struct DrainRecord {
    pub cancel_requested: Option<Time>,
    pub drain_started: Option<Time>,
    pub finalizers_complete: Option<Time>,
    pub fully_drained: Option<Time>,
}

impl LoserDrainOracle {
    /// Called when race/select starts
    pub fn on_race_start(&mut self, race_id: RaceId, participants: Vec<TaskId>, time: Time);
    
    /// Called when race determines winner
    pub fn on_race_complete(&mut self, race_id: RaceId, winner: TaskId, time: Time);
    
    /// Called when task fully drains
    pub fn on_task_drained(&mut self, task: TaskId, time: Time);
    
    /// Verify all losers are drained before race returns
    pub fn check(&self) -> Result<(), LoserDrainViolation>;
}
```

## Violation Detection
```rust
pub struct LoserDrainViolation {
    pub race_id: RaceId,
    pub winner: TaskId,
    pub undrained_losers: Vec<(TaskId, DrainRecord)>,
    pub race_return_time: Time,
}
```

A violation occurs when:
1. Race R completes at time T
2. ∃ loser L where `fully_drained` time > T or is None

## Operations That Must Drain Losers
| Operation | Winner | Losers |
|-----------|--------|--------|
| `race(a, b)` | First to complete | Other participants |
| `select\!{...}` | Selected branch | Unselected branches |
| `quorum(M, N)` | First M successes | Remaining N-M |
| `first_ok(...)` | First success | Operations after success |
| `timeout(op, d)` | Whichever wins | Timeout or op |

## Testing the Oracle
1. **Two-way race**: Winner and loser properly handled
2. **N-way race**: Multiple losers all drained
3. **Nested races**: Inner race losers drained before outer race proceeds
4. **Loser with finalizers**: Finalizers run during drain
5. **Loser with obligations**: Obligations resolved during drain
6. **Slow drain**: Loser takes time to reach checkpoint

## Drain Timing Verification
The oracle tracks timing to ensure:
- `race_return_time > max(loser_drain_times)`
- Race does NOT return until all losers fully drained

## References
- asupersync_plan_v4.md: §5.5 Race with Loser Draining, §1.1 Non-negotiable invariants
- asupersync_v4_formal_semantics.md: RACE-LOSER-DRAIN rule

## Acceptance Criteria
- Oracle identifies each race and validates that all losers reach terminal completion before the race returns.
- Also validates losers are cancelled (or otherwise transitioned out of running) promptly.
- Produces actionable diagnostics (winner/loser ids, missing completion event).
- Deterministic and runs on every E2E scenario trace.

 {"id":"bd-4nz","title":"[EPIC-TOKIO] Codec Framework (tokio-util codecs equivalent)","description":"# Codec Framework

## Overview
Bidirectional encoding/decoding with framing support.

## Core Traits

### Decoder
```rust
pub trait Decoder {
    type Item;
    type Error;
    fn decode(&mut self, src: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<Option<Self::Item>, Self::Error>;
}
```

### Encoder
```rust
pub trait Encoder<Item> {
    type Error;
    fn encode(&mut self, item: Item, dst: &mut BytesMut) -> Result<(), Self::Error>;
}
```

## Standard Codecs

### 1. LinesCodec
- Newline-delimited text
- Configurable max line length

### 2. LengthDelimitedCodec
- Length-prefixed framing
- Configurable length field (u8, u16, u32, u64)
- Big/little endian

### 3. BytesCodec
- Raw bytes passthrough

### 4. AnyDelimiterCodec
- Custom delimiter support

## Framed Transport

### FramedRead<T, D>
- Combines AsyncRead + Decoder
- Implements Stream

### FramedWrite<T, E>
- Combines AsyncWrite + Encoder
- Implements Sink

### Framed<T, U>
- Full duplex codec transport
- Stream + Sink

## Cancel-Safety
- Decode: partial frame preserved in buffer
- Encode: atomic frame writes
- Framed: tracks partial state

## Bytes Integration
- bytes::Bytes and BytesMut
- Zero-copy where possible
